summary: I was born and raised in Ethiopia, specifically in the city of Nazret. Ethiopia is a beautiful country with diverse landscapes, and my family and I lived there before I moved abroad. My parents, Nasir Ahmed and Shetu Mohammed, along with my siblings, still live there. Leaving Ethiopia in 2007 was difficult because I had to leave my close-knit family and culture behind.

My family belongs to the Gurage ethnic group in Ethiopia. Growing up, I learned a lot about Gurage culture from my father and aunt, especially about our traditions and foods. The Gurage people have unique marriage customs, like the way boys formally request a girl's hand. Our family is very close, and the Gurage language and traditions have always been a big part of my identity.

Nazret, also called Adama, is where I grew up. It’s a city in central Ethiopia, southeast of Addis Ababa, surrounded by mountains and changing colors with the seasons. I have many fond memories of living there with my family on the same property as my brother. Nazret was a beautiful place that shaped much of my early life.

Addis Ababa is the capital city of Ethiopia and is located northwest of my hometown Nazret. While I didn’t live there, it’s an important reference point for where Nazret is situated. Addis Ababa represents the larger urban center near my roots and the country I come from.

In 2007, I traveled to Qatar and stayed there for four years before moving to the United States. My time in Qatar was a significant transition period away from Ethiopia and my family. It was an important step in my journey before settling in America.

I came to the United States in 2011 after living in Qatar. My journey in the U.S. started with a brief stay in Ohio, then I moved to Dallas, Texas, and now I live in Minnesota. The U.S. has been my home for over a decade, but I still carry my Ethiopian and Gurage heritage with me.

When I first arrived in the United States in 2011, I stayed in Ohio for about 20 days. It was a short stop on my way to Dallas, Texas. Ohio was my first experience living in the U.S., which was both exciting and challenging.

After Ohio, I moved to Dallas, Texas, where I lived for almost three years. Dallas was where I started to settle more into life in America. It was a time of adjustment and growth before I eventually moved to Minnesota.
Currently, I live in Minnesota, where I have been since leaving Dallas. Minnesota has become my new home in the United States. Even though I am far from Ethiopia, I keep my culture alive here.

My father’s name is Nasir Ahmed, and he still lives in Ethiopia with my mother and siblings. I am very close to him; he often shared stories about his family and loved to joke. His stories helped me understand our Gurage culture and kept me connected to my roots even when I was far away.

My mother is Shetu Mohammed, and she remains in Ethiopia with my father and siblings. Though I am far away, I think of her often and miss my family deeply. She is an important part of my life and upbringing in the Gurage culture.

Kitfo is one of the traditional Gurage foods I always remember from my childhood in Ethiopia. My family would gather together to eat kitfo along with other dishes during celebrations and family times. It’s a dish that brings back warm memories of home and togetherness.

Kocho is another traditional food from my Gurage heritage that I grew up eating. It was always part of our family meals and celebrations back in Ethiopia. Kocho reminds me of the close family bonds and cultural traditions we shared.

Gomen, a traditional Ethiopian dish, was often served at family gatherings in my Gurage household. Eating gomen with my family was a joyful experience that connected me to my culture. I miss those times and the special flavors of home.
